content:
9 min read
Just now

--

Every performance optimization story usually starts the same way.
Someone blames LINQ.
Someone else blames the garbage collector.
Then the database gets optimized.
Caching is added.
More CPU cores are provisioned.
Yet the application is still slower than expected.
We experienced something similar while profiling a high-throughput service that processed tens of millions of telemetry records every day.
The bottleneck wasn’t network latency.
It wasn’t Entity Framework.
It wasn’t JSON serialization.
Instead, the profiler showed something unexpected:
The runtime was spending a surprising amount of time copying large value types.
No algorithm was wrong.
No memory leak existed.
The application was simply doing unnecessary work.
The solution wasn’t a new library or a sophisticated optimization technique.
It was understanding one of the oldest — and most misunderstood — features in C#:
ref
Most developers know the keyword exists, but very few understand when it genuinely improves performance, when in is the better…
